title: Quiz: Risk Greeks and Sensitivities
description: Test your understanding of option Greeks and risk measurement

questions.question: Which Greek measures sensitivity to interest rate changes?
questions.explanation: Rho (ρ) measures the sensitivity of option value to changes in the risk-free interest rate. It's most significant for long-dated options where interest rate exposure matters.

questions.question: What does gamma measure?

options.text: Rate of change of option value with spot
Rate of change of delta with spot
Rate of change of vega with spot
Rate of change of option value with time

questions.explanation: Gamma (Γ) is the second derivative of option value with respect to spot price - equivalently, it's how quickly delta changes as the underlying moves.

questions.question: Theta is typically negative for long options because:

options.text: Options lose time value as expiry approaches

questions.explanation: Theta represents time decay - options lose time value as expiration approaches. Long option holders pay for this optionality through theta decay.

questions.question: What is vanna?

options.text: Second derivative of option value with respect to spot
Third derivative with respect to spot

questions.explanation: Vanna measures the cross-sensitivity between delta and volatility (∂Δ/∂σ = ∂ν/∂S). It shows how delta changes when volatility changes, or equivalently, how vega changes with spot.

questions.question: At-the-money options have the highest gamma.
questions.explanation: True. Gamma is highest for at-the-money options, especially near expiry. This is because ATM options have delta around 0.5, which changes most rapidly with spot price movements.

questions.question: Why use numerical Greeks instead of analytical formulas?

options.text: Exotic options may not have closed-form Greek formulas
They work only for European options

questions.explanation: Numerical Greeks (finite differences) are used when analytical formulas don't exist, such as for exotic options, American options, or complex structured products.

questions.question: Portfolio delta is calculated by:
questions.explanation: Portfolio Greeks are additive - portfolio delta equals the sum of each position's delta (delta per contract × quantity). This allows straightforward aggregation across positions.

questions.question: Which are second-order Greeks? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Gamma (∂²V/∂S²), Vanna (∂²V/∂S∂σ), and Volga/Vomma (∂²V/∂σ²) are all second-order Greeks. Delta is first-order (∂V/∂S).
